summary: DHH condemns the growing adoption of employee surveillance software in remote work environments, calling it a sign of managerial failure. He argues that while early pandemic panic might have excused the impulse, two years later managers can no longer claim ignorance. He points to Basecamp's decision to ban surveillance integrations and notes that half of tech workers would quit rather than submit to monitoring. The post advocates for evaluating work quality rather than tracking screen time, arguing that surveillance destroys the mutual benefits that make remote work valuable for both employers and employees.
key_points: Employee surveillance software reveals managerial insecurity and incompetence rather than solving productivity problems
The pandemic excuse for adopting surveillance tools has expired — continued use reflects who these managers truly are
Remote work's value proposition is a two-sided deal: productivity for employers, flexibility for employees — surveillance poisons both sides
Managers must transition from line-of-sight supervision to evaluating quality of work output
Half of tech workers say they'd resign rather than accept surveillance, though economic downturns may weaken that resolve
money_quotes: Employee surveillance software is managerial bankruptcy.
Moving to remote work has brought out the worst in some managers. It's revealed their insecurities and paranoia, and caused them to address these in a spectacle of incompetence.

Two years after those frantic first months of the pandemic, you can't claim ignorance or fear as excusable reasons to carry on like this. Now you have to accept that if you're a manager reaching for surveillance systems to supervise employees, this is just who you are.
These dreadful surveillance tools seek to entrench the former. They try to recreate management by counting hours in the seat in front of the screen – a method that never worked to begin with!

Remote work might have seemed like a mixed blessing during the pandemic, but it's a real breakthrough in work-life relations during more normal times. Don't you dare spoil the bargain with this surveillance poison bullshit.
key_insight: Managers who reach for surveillance tools to monitor remote workers are admitting they cannot evaluate the quality of work itself — the only metric that ever mattered.
